critic_raw:
Round 1 Critique:

1. (accuracy_hides_rare_defect_performance) 1.4% prevalence renders overall accuracy meaningless for defect detection quality.

2. (metric_not_aligned_with_asymmetric_costs) The paper's own discussion acknowledges asymmetric costs. The evaluation metric contradicts this.

3. (operating_point_omitted) No defect-class performance metric reported at any threshold. The paper recommends deployment to reduce escapes without evidence this will happen.
defender_raw:
Round 1 Defense:

1. The absolute performance is strong: 99.1% is high accuracy even in absolute terms.
2. The augmentation techniques (brightness normalization, geometric augmentation) are appropriate.
3. Multiple random seeds confirm reproducibility. The model is technically sound.
adjudication_raw:
After Round 1:
All three points remain OPEN. The defender is defending the model's technical construction (augmentation, seeds) rather than the metric's validity for the stated use case. These are non-responsive rebuttals.

Round 2:
Critic Mode 2 on Point 1: 99.1% accuracy in a setting with 1.4% defects provides almost no information about whether defects are detected. A model detecting zero defects would achieve 98.6% — only 0.5 pp below the new model. The paper cannot distinguish a good defect detector from a near-trivial classifier on this metric alone.

Defender Mode 2 on Point 1: The model was trained with class labels and presumably with defect-positive examples. It would be unusual for a trained classifier to completely ignore its positive class. The model likely detects some defects — the issue is that we don't know how many.

Orchestrator: 'Likely detects some defects' is not a basis for recommending deployment to reduce escapes. The deployment claim requires evidence. The defender concedes we don't know how many defects are caught — which is exactly the problem.

All three points force-resolved: critique sustained.

Final verdict: critique_wins
